---
title: "New Customer Onboarding – Insights from the First Week"
author: "Andrei"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE)
Sys.setlocale("LC_ALL", "nb_NO.UTF-8")  # keep Norwegian locale so special characters / number formats in the source file parse correctly
```

# Load required packages

```{r message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(janitor)
library(skimr)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)
library(purrr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
options(scipen = 999)
```

# Import the dataset and inspect variable names, data types and structure

```{r import-data, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
data_raw <- read_excel(
  "First_week_anonymous.xlsx",
  na = c("", "NA")
)

# Translate the original Norwegian column-name patterns to English for readability.
# Source columns follow the pattern PLAYDAY_<1-7>_<GAME TYPE>_<TURNOVER|LOSS>.
data_raw <- data_raw %>%
  rename_with(~ .x %>%
    str_replace_all("ALDER", "AGE") %>%
    str_replace_all("SPILLDAG", "PLAYDAY") %>%
    str_replace_all("OMSETNING", "TURNOVER") %>%
    str_replace_all("TAP", "LOSS") %>%
    str_replace_all("KASINO", "CASINO") %>%
    str_replace_all("SPORT", "SPORTS") %>%
    str_replace_all("LOTTERI", "LOTTERY") %>%
    str_replace_all("ØYEBLIKKSPILL", "INSTANT_GAMES")
  )

data <- data_raw %>%
  mutate(
    across(
      4:59,
      ~ {
        x <- as.character(.)
        x <- str_replace_all(x, "[−–—]", "-")  # normalize Unicode minus signs
        round(
          parse_number(
            x,
            locale = locale(decimal_mark = ".", grouping_mark = ",")
          ),
          0
        )
      }
    )
  )
# All variables were first converted to text, then parsed as numbers using locale-aware
# parsing to ensure consistency. After numeric parsing, monetary variables were rounded
# to whole numbers to remove unnecessary decimal precision.

glimpse(data) # check
skim(data)    # check

vars_na <- c(
  "PLAYDAY_4_SPORTS_LOSS",
  "PLAYDAY_6_SPORTS_LOSS",
  "PLAYDAY_5_INSTANT_GAMES_LOSS"
)

data_clean <- data %>%
  filter(if_all(all_of(vars_na), ~ !is.na(.)))

# Rows with missing values in selected day- and game-specific variables were removed
# from the analysis dataset.
skim(data_clean) # final data check
```

# Transform raw daily variables into behavioral features / create variables suitable for EDA, risk assessment and segmentation

```{r}
# Covers day 1 to 7, i.e. the customer's first week.
# Automatically identifies all variables related to:
# - Turnover (stake)
# - Loss / win from the customer's perspective

playdays <- 1:7 # all 7 days
turnover_vars <- grep("TURNOVER", names(data_clean), value = TRUE)  # stake
loss_vars     <- grep("LOSS", names(data_clean), value = TRUE)      # net loss (customer perspective)

# Total turnover and total loss
data_feat <- data_clean %>%
  mutate(
    total_turnover = rowSums(select(., all_of(turnover_vars))),
    total_loss      = rowSums(select(., all_of(loss_vars)))
  )

# Sums all stakes across days and game types into total turnover per customer.
# Sums all daily losses/wins into total net loss or win per customer.
# Lifts the data from day-level to customer-level, which is necessary for segmentation.

# Aggregate by game type, e.g. Casino, Sports, Lottery, Instant games

data_feat <- data_feat %>%
  mutate(
    casino_turnover  = rowSums(select(., contains("CASINO_TURNOVER"))),
    sports_turnover  = rowSums(select(., contains("SPORTS_TURNOVER"))),
    lottery_turnover = rowSums(select(., contains("LOTTERY_TURNOVER"))),
    instant_turnover = rowSums(select(., contains("INSTANT_GAMES_TURNOVER")))
  )

# Splits total turnover by game type.
# Gives insight into what the customer plays, not just how much.

# Create behavioral shares (key for segmentation)
data_feat <- data_feat %>%
  mutate(
    casino_share  = casino_turnover / total_turnover,
    sports_share  = sports_turnover / total_turnover,
    lottery_share = lottery_turnover / total_turnover,
    instant_share = instant_turnover / total_turnover
  )

# Converts absolute amounts into shares of total stake.
# A customer can have low turnover but still a high casino share.
# Shares make it possible to compare customers with different spending levels fairly.

# Active days (any stake > 0)
data_feat <- data_feat %>%
  mutate(
    active_days = rowSums(
      select(., all_of(turnover_vars)) > 0
    )
  )

# Counts how many of the 7 days the customer had at least one stake.
# Measures play frequency and engagement, independent of amount and game type.

# Early risk indicators (case-relevant)
data_feat <- data_feat %>%
  mutate(
    high_turnover      = total_turnover > quantile(total_turnover, 0.90),
    high_casino_share   = casino_share > 0.5,
    young_male          = (AGE <= 25 & GENDER == "M")
  )

# Identifies customers with:
# - Very high turnover (top 10%)
# - High casino share
# - Young male, a known risk group
```

# Exploratory data analysis

```{r}

# Age distribution
summary(data_feat$AGE) # youngest customer 19 years - oldest 99 - average age ~34

# Gender distribution
table(data_feat$GENDER)        # 212,374 M and 152,772 F
prop.table(table(data_feat$GENDER)) # 58% M and 42% F

# Game type preferences
summary(data_feat$casino_share)
summary(data_feat$sports_share)
summary(data_feat$lottery_share)
summary(data_feat$instant_share)

# A large share of customers do not play casino, sports, or instant games in the first week.
# Customers who play lottery often play only lottery, giving them a very high lottery share.
# This pulls the average lottery share strongly upward.
# Lottery has the highest average turnover share.

# Visualization
## Distribution of total turnover (log scale)
mean_turnover   <- mean(data_feat$total_turnover)
median_turnover <- median(data_feat$total_turnover)
# Black dashed line → mean
# Yellow line → median
ggplot(data_feat, aes(total_turnover)) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 50,
    fill = "#C8102E",
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(
    breaks = c(10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 10000, 100000),
    labels = scales::comma
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(0, 60000, by = 10000),
    labels = scales::comma
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = median_turnover,
    color = "#FFFF00",
    size = 1.1
  ) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = mean_turnover,
    color = "black",
    linetype = "dashed",
    size = 1
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of total turnover (log scale)",
    x = "Total turnover (log)",
    y = "Number of customers"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
  )

# The figure shows the distribution of total turnover per customer in the first week.
# The x-axis uses a log scale to make large differences in spending readable.
# Most customers have low turnover, while a small minority have very high turnover.
#
# The log scale makes it possible to see the full distribution.
# 50% of customers have lower turnover than the median.
# The mean is strongly affected by customers with high turnover and sits clearly to
# the right of the median — a classic sign of a right-skewed distribution with a few
# customers spending much more than the rest.

data_long <- data_feat %>%
  select(ROW_NUM, casino_share, sports_share, lottery_share, instant_share) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -ROW_NUM,
    names_to = "game_type",
    values_to = "share"
  )

data_feat <- data_feat %>%
  mutate(
    age_group = case_when(
      AGE <= 35 ~ "19–35 years",
      TRUE      ~ "36+ years"
    )
  )

# Game type dominance – violin plot only
ggplot(data_long, aes(x = game_type, y = share, fill = game_type)) +
  geom_violin(
    trim = FALSE,
    alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      casino_share  = "#C8102E",  # red
      lottery_share = "#2E8B57",  # green
      instant_share = "#F2C300",  # yellow
      sports_share  = "#1F77B4"   # blue
    ),
    labels = c(
      casino_share  = "Casino",
      lottery_share = "Lottery",
      instant_share = "Instant games",
      sports_share  = "Sports"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Share of total turnover by game type",
    x = "Game type",
    y = "Share of total turnover"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

# Lottery: broad usage, often a dominant share, low risk
# Sports: mid position, moderate intensity, event-driven
# Instant games: short, intense sessions
# Casino: played by few, high intensity when played, higher risk
#
# Casino and instant games show concentrated usage — at least 75% of customers do not
# play these game types in the first week. The violin plot confirms this: the majority
# of customers have a zero share for these game types.

# Scatter plot: Age × Lottery share
ggplot(data_feat, aes(AGE, lottery_share, color = age_group)) +
  geom_jitter(
    alpha = 0.3,
    size = 1.6,
    width = 0.5,
    height = 0
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "19–35 years" = "#1F77B4",
      "36+ years"   = "#2E8B57"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Lottery share by age",
    x = "Age",
    y = "Share of total turnover spent on lottery",
    color = "Age"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
  )

# Both the 19–35 and 36+ age groups show many customers near a share of 1 (lottery only)
# and many near 0 (little or no lottery), with no clear age gradient. Lottery is a
# broadly used product regardless of age, and appears as a low-risk product in the
# onboarding phase.

# Age × Sports share
ggplot(data_feat, aes(AGE, sports_share, color = age_group)) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.15, width = 0.5, height = 0) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "19–35 years" = "#C8102E",  # red
      "36+ years"   = "#7F7F7F"   # gray
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Sports share by age",
    x = "Age",
    y = "Share of total turnover spent on sports",
    color = "Age"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
  )

# Sports betting is more concentrated among customers aged 19–35, with significant
# overlap between age groups but a center of gravity skewed toward younger customers.
# Sports betting shows a moderate risk profile, with somewhat higher concentration
# among younger customers.

data_feat <- data_feat %>%
  mutate(
    young_male_19_25 = case_when(
      GENDER == "M" & AGE >= 19 & AGE <= 25 ~ "Young men (19–25)",
      GENDER == "M"                          ~ "Men 26+",
      TRUE                                   ~ "Women"
    )
  )

# Young men (19–25) → highest known risk
# Men 26+ → elevated risk
# Women → lower risk on average

ggplot(data_feat, aes(
    x = factor(
      young_male_19_25,
      levels = c("Young men (19–25)", "Men 26+", "Women")
    ),
    y = casino_share,
    fill = young_male_19_25
  )
) +
  geom_violin(trim = FALSE, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Young men (19–25)" = "#C8102E",
      "Men 26+"            = "#1F77B4",
      "Women"              = "#FFD700"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Casino share by gender and age",
    x = "",
    y = "Share of total turnover spent on casino"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

# Casino behavior is strongly concentrated among young men aged 19–25. Women and
# older men play casino to a very limited extent in the first week, while young men
# show both a higher share and greater variation in casino exposure.
```

# Customer segmentation (k-means) – prepare data for clustering

```{r}
data_feat <- data_feat %>%
  mutate(
    casino_share = ifelse(is.na(casino_share), 0, casino_share),
    sports_share = ifelse(is.na(sports_share), 0, sports_share)
  )

# Some customers had zero turnover in the first week, which produces missing shares
# when calculating game shares. For segmentation, these were treated as a zero share,
# since the customer has no exposure to that game type.

# Standardize variables (critical for k-means)
cluster_data <- data_feat %>%
  select(
    AGE,
    total_turnover,
    active_days,
    casino_share,
    sports_share
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    total_turnover = log1p(total_turnover)
  ) %>%
  scale()

colSums(is.na(cluster_data))  # 1,008 customers had no activity at all in the first
                               # week BEFORE this step; now 0 after the NA handling above

# Choose number of clusters (elbow method)
set.seed(123)

wss <- map_dbl(2:8, function(k) {
  kmeans(cluster_data, centers = k, nstart = 20)$tot.withinss
})

plot(
  2:8, wss, type = "b",
  xlab = "Number of clusters",
  ylab = "Within-cluster sum of squares",
  main = "Elbow method"
)

# Run k-means
set.seed(123)

k4 <- kmeans(cluster_data, centers = 4, nstart = 50)

data_seg <- data_feat %>%
  mutate(segment = factor(k4$cluster))

# Profile the segments
segment_profile <- data_seg %>%
  group_by(segment) %>%
  summarise(
    n_customers        = n(),
    mean_age           = mean(AGE),
    mean_turnover      = mean(total_turnover),
    mean_active_days   = mean(active_days),
    mean_casino_share  = mean(casino_share),
    share_male         = mean(GENDER == "M")
  )

segment_profile

segment_table <- data_seg %>%
  group_by(segment) %>%
  summarise(
    N_customers       = n(),
    Mean_age          = mean(AGE),
    Mean_turnover     = mean(total_turnover),
    Mean_active_days  = mean(active_days),
    Mean_casino_share = mean(casino_share),
    Share_male        = mean(GENDER == "M")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    # Round for readability
    Mean_age          = round(Mean_age, 1),
    Mean_turnover     = round(Mean_turnover, 0),
    Mean_active_days  = round(Mean_active_days, 1),
    Mean_casino_share = round(Mean_casino_share, 3),
    Share_male        = round(Share_male, 2),

    # Segment labels
    Segment_name = case_when(
      segment == 1 ~ "Mature low-intensity players",
      segment == 2 ~ "Young engaged men",
      segment == 3 ~ "Young exploring customers",
      segment == 4 ~ "Young high-intensity casino players"
    ),

    # Risk level
    Risk = case_when(
      segment == 1 ~ "Low",
      segment == 2 ~ "Moderate",
      segment == 3 ~ "Low",
      segment == 4 ~ "High"
    )
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Segment = segment
  ) %>%
  select(
    Segment,
    Segment_name,
    Risk,
    N_customers,
    Mean_age,
    Mean_turnover,
    Mean_active_days,
    Mean_casino_share,
    Share_male
  )

# Table 1 shows four distinct customer segments with large differences in age,
# activity level, and risk profile.

segment_table_final <- segment_table %>%
  rename(
    Segment          = Segment,
    Segment_name     = Segment_name,
    Risk             = Risk,
    N_customers      = N_customers,
    Avg_age          = Mean_age,
    Avg_turnover     = Mean_turnover,
    Avg_active_days  = Mean_active_days,
    Avg_casino_share = Mean_casino_share,
    Share_male       = Share_male
  )
```

```{r}

# Run PCA
pca <- prcomp(cluster_data, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)

# Add PCA coordinates and segment labels to the data
pca_df <- data_seg %>%
  mutate(
    PC1 = pca$x[,1],
    PC2 = pca$x[,2],
    Segment_name = case_when(
      segment == 1 ~ "Mature low-intensity players",
      segment == 2 ~ "Young engaged men",
      segment == 3 ~ "Young exploring customers",
      segment == 4 ~ "Young high-intensity casino players"
    )
  )

ggplot(pca_df, aes(PC1, PC2, color = Segment_name)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Customer segments based on first-week play behavior",
    x = "Behavioral dimension 1 (PCA)",
    y = "Behavioral dimension 2 (PCA)",
    color = "Segment"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
  )
```
